ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general laksa entry (FOODON_03311069); no specific deluxe seafood variant with scallop, prawn and clam exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific laksa code. A0FKP (Asian-style noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for laksa or this specific seafood noodle soup variant.

main_ingredients: laksa broth (coconut milk, spice paste, dried shrimp)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Laksa Deluxe with scallops, prawns and clams is approximately 500 g, inclusive of noodles, broth, seafood toppings, bean sprouts and tofu puffs.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa, with coconut milk — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Singapore laksa (coconut milk-based). The Deluxe variant adds scallops, extra prawns and clams, which increases protein and modestly increases sodium relative to the base HPB entry. Per-100g values are adapted from HPB laksa data with upward protein adjustment (~+1.5 g/100g) to reflect the additional seafood load. All other macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the HPB base entry. Adaptation is documented; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008); Laksa analogue estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Laksa Deluxe with scallop, prawn and clam. GI estimated at 47 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice vermicelli noodles (GI ~53 for boiled rice noodles per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the high fat and protein content of the coconut milk broth and seafood, which is known to lower the effective GI of the meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (47 × 49 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 22.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 9.8 g/100g × 500 g = 49 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~2100 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Laksa Deluxe due to its very high sodium content (~2100 mg per standard 500 g bowl), which represents approximately 91% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; portion reduction and requesting reduced-salt broth are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~23 per bowl) and high saturated fat from coconut milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Laksa Deluxe in moderation; the estimated GI of 47 (low) is partially offset by a moderate glycemic load of ~23 per bowl due to the large serving size, and the high saturated fat content from coconut milk may contribute to insulin resistance with frequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Laksa Deluxe due to its high saturated fat content (~2.8 g/100g, predominantly from coconut milk) and very high sodium (~2100 mg per bowl), both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional consumption with smaller portions is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa Deluxe (Scallop, Prawn and Clam) is a premium variant of Singapore laksa, one of the most iconic dishes in Singaporean cuisine. It consists of rice vermicelli noodles served in a rich, spiced coconut milk broth made from a rempah (spice paste) of dried shrimp, lemongrass, galangal, candlenut, turmeric and chilli. The Deluxe version is distinguished by its generous seafood topping of whole scallops, large prawns and clams, in addition to the standard accompaniments of tofu puffs, bean sprouts, a hard-boiled egg, and fresh laksa leaves (Vietnamese coriander). The broth is deeply aromatic, creamy and mildly spicy, with a characteristic orange-red colour from the spice paste and coconut milk. The texture is rich and silky from the coconut milk, with the seafood providing a sweet, briny contrast. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 475 kcal, 32.5 g protein, 21 g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut milk), and 49 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at approximately 2100 mg per bowl, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is 47 (low), moderated by the fat and protein content, but the glycemic load per bowl is moderate at approximately 23. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12 and zinc from the seafood. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains crustaceans, molluscs and soy allergens. Laksa is commonly consumed as a main meal at hawker centres, food courts and casual dining restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in laksa deluxe?
Laksa with scallop prawn clam calories

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Singapore laksa (coconut milk-based), which is the closest approved primary source. The Deluxe variant with scallops, extra prawns and clams is not individually listed in HPB Foodo; values have been adapted with a documented protein upward adjustment to reflect the additional seafood. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by the fat and protein content of the dish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo laksa (coconut milk) base entry with a protein adjustment of approximately +1.5 g/100g to reflect the additional scallop, prawn and clam load in the Deluxe variant. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for laksa. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for laksa. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for laksa. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in coconut-milk-based dishes per HPB data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (47) based on rice vermicelli noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source, moderated by coconut milk fat and seafood protein; source is University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. (2008). 7. Serving size of 500 g is a standard bowl estimate consistent with HPB Singapore portion guidance for laksa. 8. The food is served by Qi Ji - Funan on field survey data at SGD 13.00. 9. Halal status is assessed as likely halal based on the seafood and plant-based ingredients; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 10. Allergens include crustaceans (prawns, shrimp in paste), molluscs (scallops, clams), and soy (tofu puffs); gluten may be present depending on noodle formulation.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Adapted nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo laksa base entry with documented protein adjustment for Deluxe seafood variant.
Estimated GI at 47 using University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle data with documented analogous estimate basis.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Used only allowed match_type values (closest_parent, none) for ontology fields.
Set confidence to medium reflecting adapted rather than exact source match.
